title: Quantum dynamics of two $XX$ interacting PT-symmetric non-Hermitian qubits: enhancement of quantum annealing
authors: Yana Komissarova, Mikhail V. Fistul, Ilya M. Eremin
abstract: Quantum information platforms enable analog quantum simulations, such as quantum annealing, offering a promising route to solving complex combinatorial optimization problems. Here, we propose a quantum information architecture based on networks of interacting parity-time (PT)-symmetric non-Hermitian qubits. While the dynamics of individual PT-symmetric qubits have been experimentally demonstrated across multiple platforms including NV centers, superconducting circuits, and trapped-ion systems yet coherent dynamics in interacting systems remain largely unexplored. To address this issue we theoretically investigate stationary and time-dependent Hamiltonians relevant to quantum annealing using a minimal model of two interacting XX-coupled PT-symmetric non-Hermitian qubits. We analyze both symmetry-preserving and symmetry-broken regimes and demonstrate that adding even tiny PT-symmetric non-Hermitian terms in the qubits Hamiltonian allows to greatly enhance the probability of reaching the ground state after annealing.
summary: This paper explores how PT-symmetric non-Hermiticity can be used to enhance quantum adiabatic annealing. By introducing a small amount of gain and loss into a two-qubit system, the researchers show that the probability of reaching the target ground state can be significantly increased compared to the standard Hermitian case.
main_problem: Investigating whether introducing PT-symmetric non-Hermitian terms into the Hamiltonian can enhance the efficiency of Quantum Adiabatic Annealing (QAA) by mitigating the effects of small energy gaps.
main_result: The study demonstrates that adding even small PT-symmetric non-Hermitian terms can significantly enhance the probability of reaching the ground state during annealing, effectively mitigating Landau-Zener-Stuckelberg tunneling failures.
method: Theoretical analysis of stationary and time-dependent Hamiltonians using a 4x4 matrix approach, reduction to an effective 2x2 model, and numerical solution of the time-dependent Schrodinger equation.
model_or_system: A minimal model consisting of two interacting XX-coupled PT-symmetric non-Hermitian qubits subject to a time-dependent annealing Hamiltonian with a staggered imaginary longitudinal magnetic field.
key_observables: Probability of reaching the ground state (P_gr), population probabilities (P_up, P_down), and the energy spectrum (real and imaginary parts of eigenvalues).
important_parameters: Gain/loss parameter (gamma), annealing speed (k), qubit bias (epsilon), interaction strength (g), and transition amplitude (Delta).
main_assumptions: The study uses a minimal two-qubit model, assumes all individual qubits are identical, and assumes the non-Hermitian part of the Hamiltonian remains constant during the annealing process.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of the two-qubit system; Figure 2 displays the real part of energy levels with color gradients for the imaginary part; Figure 5 shows energy differences in different PT phases; Figure 6 illustrates the failure of standard Hermitian QAA.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of QAA efficiency, defines the non-Hermitian two-qubit model, analyzes the energy spectrum and phase transitions (PT-unbroken vs. broken), investigates time-independent and time-dependent dynamics, and concludes with the derivation of the enhanced success probability.
why_interesting: It provides a novel way to use non-Hermitian physics (gain and loss) as a resource to improve the performance of quantum annealing protocols, which is highly relevant for open quantum systems and quantum control.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Schematic representation of two PT –symmetric in- teracting qubits. A transition amplitude between the eigen- states, ∆, a qubit’s bias, ϵ, a staggered gain (loss), γ, and an exchange interaction between the qubits, g, are indicated.

FIG. 2: Calculated normalized energy spectrum Re[E/∆] as a function of the external parameter s for g/∆= 1 and ϵ/∆= 0: (a) γ/∆= 0, (b) γ/∆= 0.1; and for ϵ/∆= 0.9: (c) γ/∆= 0, (d) γ/∆= 0.1; and for ϵ/∆= 1.1: (e) γ/∆= 0, (f) γ/∆= 0.2, (g) γ/∆= 0.3, (h) γ/∆= 0.5. Black curves refer to the real energy eigenvalues, while colored lines correspond to eigenvalues with nonzero imaginary parts. For the colored curves, the real part of the eigenvalue determines the position of the colored line, whereas the imaginary part is encoded in the color gradient, ranging from blue (small imaginary part) to red (large imaginary part).

FIG. 3: Calculated energy spectrum of the effective Hamiltonian (12) for (a,b) ϵ/∆= 0, g/∆= 1 and scr = 0.414: (a) γ/∆= 0, (b) γ/∆= 0.1; and (c,d) ϵ/∆= 0.9, g/∆= 1 and scr = 0.619: (c) γ/∆= 0, (d) γ/∆= 0.1. The color encoding is the same as in Fig. 2.

FIG. 4: Calculated time evolution of the relative population probabilities P↑(↓)(t) of the basis states, | ↑⟩and | ↓⟩, for fixed values of ˜s0: (a) ˜s0 = ±0.2, (b) ˜s0 = ±0.08, (c) ˜s0 = 0. Other parameters were chosen as ϵ/∆= 0, g/∆= 1 and γ/∆= 0.1.
FIG. 5: The energy difference between exact eigenvalues of the effective Hamiltonian (12) for (a) PT -symmetric phase, (b) PT -symmetry-broken phase.
